jokes.text: That can't be good.
jokes.setup: Michael hears rattling from underneath his house
jokes.punchline: His understated reaction to what's clearly a major structural problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael Bluth reaction - his characteristic understatement in the face of disaster. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: You know this isn't a real house? It's a model house.
jokes.setup: Inspector discovers serious plumbing problems
jokes.punchline: Michael's matter-of-fact reminder that they're living in a fake display house
jokes.explanation: Establishes the show's absurd premise while highlighting how the family's circumstances have normalized the bizarre. Great character moment for Michael's practical acceptance of chaos.

jokes.text: Plus, there's a lot of blue paint down there. And some pieces of denim?
jokes.setup: Inspector discovers mysterious debris under the house
jokes.punchline: The blue paint and denim clearly reference Tobias's Blue Man Group obsession
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual callback that rewards longtime viewers while setting up the episode's later Blue Man Group plot. The denim detail makes it unmistakably Tobias.

jokes.text: I believe there's a freeloading loser in my bed.
jokes.setup: Michael assumes Lucille means Buster when she complains about someone in her bed
jokes.punchline: She's actually referring to Oscar, creating confusion about which family member she's insulting
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille venom delivered with perfect ambiguity. Shows her willingness to insult anyone while creating genuine confusion about her target.

jokes.text: Buster! The Cornballer! [Both Screaming] [Sizzling]
jokes.setup: Oscar forgets Buster has a prosthetic hand and tries to save him from the dangerous device
jokes.punchline: The Cornballer burns them both, continuing its reign of kitchen terror
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to the show's most dangerous running gag. The irony that Oscar gets hurt trying to prevent Buster from getting hurt is classic AD layering.

jokes.text: The only reason she likes him is he has hair. And the only reason he has any is because he's never had any stress.
jokes.setup: George Sr. complains about Lucille preferring Oscar
jokes.punchline: His bitter observation that Oscar's stress-free life has preserved his hair while George Sr.'s criminal lifestyle has cost him his
jokes.explanation: Sharp character insight disguised as petty complaint. Reveals George Sr.'s jealousy while highlighting the brothers' different life choices through hair metaphor.

jokes.text: Hey, man! Yeah!
jokes.setup: George Sr. is ranting about not wanting to return to prison
jokes.punchline: He suddenly adopts hippie slang mid-complaint, forgetting he's supposed to be the serious twin
jokes.explanation: Subtle character beat showing George Sr. unconsciously adopting Oscar's mannerisms while hiding in his place. Shows how identity becomes fluid in the twins' world.

jokes.text: Remember that French movie we tried to sneak into once- Dangerous Cousins? No, l-Why? Did we- [Stammering] I don't remember that, no.
jokes.setup: Maeby casually mentions a French film about cousins
jokes.punchline: George Michael's panicked denial reveals his guilt about their relationship while Maeby remains oblivious
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of George Michael's tortured attraction to Maeby. His obvious panic contrasts brilliantly with her casual tone, creating classic cringe comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael remembered very well. In fact, he currently had a copy of the DVD hidden in his sock drawer.
jokes.setup: George Michael claims not to remember the French cousin movie
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals he not only remembers but owns it and hides it like pornography
jokes.explanation: Brilliant narrator intervention that exposes George Michael's lie while adding the perfect detail about treating it like contraband. The sock drawer detail is painfully accurate for a teenage boy.

jokes.text: The studio at which she'd finagled a job had hoped to avoid controversy by artlessly explaining that the two leads weren't biological cousins.
jokes.setup: The studio wants to avoid controversy about the cousin relationship
jokes.punchline: Their 'solution' is transparently artificial and makes the subtext text, exactly like AD itself does
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-commentary on AD's own approach to the George Michael/Maeby storyline. The word 'artlessly' perfectly captures both the fictional studio's and the real show's deliberately clumsy handling of taboo subjects.

jokes.text: But it's only 71 minutes long. And it's only, like, 63 minutes long.
jokes.setup: The studio wants Maeby to cut down the already short movie
jokes.punchline: The runtime keeps shrinking in the same conversation, suggesting it's being butchered in real time
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation joke showing the movie getting shorter even as they discuss it. Captures the panic of studio interference and the arbitrary nature of executive decisions.

jokes.text: I mean, if it maintains any of the complex eroticism of the French original anyway.
jokes.setup: George Michael claims the movie isn't Ann's type
jokes.punchline: He accidentally reveals deep familiarity with the erotic content while trying to sound casual
jokes.explanation: Perfect George Michael moment - trying to sound sophisticated while accidentally revealing his inappropriate interest. The phrase 'complex eroticism' is hilariously pretentious for a teenager.

jokes.text: Do I work- I developed an eating disorder being your assistant, Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael questions whether Tobias actually works there
jokes.punchline: Tobias's dramatic claim that the nonexistent job gave him an eating disorder
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Tobias logic - developing psychological symptoms from a job he never actually did. Perfect character integration showing his tendency to create dramatic narratives from nothing.

jokes.text: I never saw you at the desk. Well, excuse me if I was too busy on my knees in front of the toilet, Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Tobias was never at his desk
jokes.punchline: Tobias's defense inadvertently sounds sexual despite referring to vomiting
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double entendre delivered with complete innocence. The callback to his history of unintentional sexual implications while maintaining story logic about bulimia.

jokes.text: I feel like a- [Bleeps] idiot.
jokes.setup: Tobias realizes he misunderstood and performed for depressed men instead of the Blue Man Group
jokes.punchline: He's covered in blue paint addressing a therapy group, looking absurd while swearing
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual payoff to the setup. Tobias covered in blue paint swearing at a depression support group is peak absurdist comedy with earned character development.

jokes.text: Oh, this is my fault too? Everything- No. No! I will not let this make me eat! [Sobbing]
jokes.setup: Michael asks why Tobias didn't tell him about Kitty's call
jokes.punchline: Tobias has a complete breakdown about food and blame in response to a simple question
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tobias's fragile psychological state. His dramatic overreaction to minor criticism fits his character while advancing the eating disorder subplot.

jokes.text: Say good-bye to these. Spring break! Whoo!
jokes.setup: Flashback to Kitty's previous meetings with Michael
jokes.punchline: She exposes herself while making inappropriate spring break references in a business context
jokes.explanation: Classic Kitty callback establishing her unhinged behavior pattern. The spring break reference adds absurdist context to her inappropriate exposure.

jokes.text: Shall I knock Dad out and chain him to a pipe somewhere? Or should I risk another herpes outbreak with Kitty?
jokes.setup: Michael weighs his options for dealing with his problems
jokes.punchline: He casually discusses kidnapping his father vs. getting an STD as equally viable choices
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny escalation showing how Michael's moral compass has shifted. Treating violence and disease as practical considerations reveals the family's corrupting influence.

jokes.text: I assumed that you were talking about the mutual fund and not a compact disc of you singing to your hand.
jokes.setup: Gob asked for money to invest in a Franklin CD
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals the misunderstanding between financial investment and Gob's ventriloquist album
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection joke that retroactively explains the money request while highlighting both characters' perspectives. Michael's practicality vs. Gob's delusions of artistic grandeur.

jokes.text: It ain't easy bein' white / It ain't easy bein' brown / All this pressure to be bright / I got childrens all over town
jokes.setup: Gob's ventriloquist act is supposed to break down racial barriers
jokes.punchline: Franklin's song contains terrible rhymes and inappropriate content about having illegitimate children
jokes.explanation: Cringe comedy gold showing Gob's tone-deaf attempt at social commentary. The terrible rhymes and inappropriate subject matter perfectly capture his misguided artistic ambitions.

jokes.text: Sometimes- Hey, where'd the guy go?
jokes.setup: Franklin is singing his racial harmony song
jokes.punchline: Franklin breaks character to ask where his ventriloquist went, breaking the fourth wall of the act
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that breaks the ventriloquist act's internal logic. Franklin acknowledging his operator's absence is both absurd and technically impossible, perfect AD surrealism.

jokes.text: Crying like a couple of girls. Aw, you're the only one crying.
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael have an emotional moment over the CD
jokes.punchline: Gob calls them both girls for crying, but only he is actually crying
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Gob's lack of self-awareness. He criticizes emotional behavior while being the most emotional one present, classic Gob obliviousness.

jokes.text: Franklin didn't want to kiss at all. Gob had soaked the puppet's mouth in ether.
jokes.setup: Franklin the puppet approaches George Sr. for a kiss
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Gob has weaponized his ventriloquist dummy with anesthetic
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and absurd twist. Gob using his puppet as a knockout device is unexpected and perfectly in character. The casual narrator delivery makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Well, maybe the fact that we don't know if we're together or not is a sign that we should split again. Or stay split up.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay discuss their relationship status
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's logic creates a loop where any relationship state leads to the same conclusion
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Lindsay's commitment to avoiding actual commitment. Her circular logic ensures she never has to make a real decision about the relationship.

jokes.text: [Imitating George Sr.] I cherish you, Gob. Come here and give me a kiss. I'm driving, Dad. [As George Sr.] Now!
jokes.setup: Gob feels guilty about betraying his father
jokes.punchline: He uses ventriloquism to make his unconscious father 'forgive' him and demand a kiss
jokes.explanation: Deeply disturbing and funny character moment. Gob manipulating his unconscious father's 'voice' for emotional validation is peak psychological dysfunction delivered through ventriloquism.

jokes.text: It's not even open. Michael!
jokes.setup: Gob notices Michael's CD gift in the car
jokes.punchline: He realizes Michael lied about listening to it since it's still sealed
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the CD subplot. The visual evidence of Michael's lie adds stakes to their earlier emotional scene and drives Gob's next actions.

jokes.text: I can tell you who it's not. It's not Bull. It's not Harry Anderson. He's white. I know who it is.
jokes.setup: Kitty says her sponsor was on Night Court but won't say who
jokes.punchline: She immediately starts eliminating people, making the secret obvious while claiming secrecy
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation comedy. Kitty's attempt at discretion immediately fails as she narrows down the options. Good character work showing her inability to keep secrets.

jokes.text: 'Oftentimes the heart acts without consulting the head, and thusly'- Oh, I see you wasted no time in filling my seat hole.
jokes.setup: Tobias enters reading something profound, then sees his desk has been taken
jokes.punchline: His complaint about his 'seat hole' being filled sounds sexual while referring to his workspace
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias double entendre delivered with complete innocence. The transition from pseudo-philosophical quote to crude-sounding complaint is expertly timed.

jokes.text: It's the last time you're going to be seeing these. How ironic. I just found out I'm staying.
jokes.setup: Kitty prepares to expose herself as a farewell gesture
jokes.punchline: Tobias announces he's staying just as she's about to flash him goodbye
jokes.explanation: Nice timing joke that creates an awkward situation. Kitty's planned farewell gesture becomes inappropriate once Tobias announces he's staying, but she's already committed.

jokes.text: Wow, you are so real. Why can't I meet someone like you?
jokes.setup: Kitty interacts with Tobias during his emotional moment
jokes.punchline: She praises his authenticity to a man who's professionally an actor and personally performs constant personas
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony since Tobias is the least authentic person imaginable. Kitty's attraction to his 'realness' when he's literally always performing shows her poor judgment in men.

jokes.text: I recently heard that the actor, John Larroquette, was looking for a meaty character piece.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Tobias and Kitty go out together
jokes.punchline: Tobias somehow connects this to a random celebrity looking for film roles
jokes.explanation: Absurd Tobias non-sequitur that connects back to the Night Court conversation. His ability to make everything about show business opportunities shows his delusional actor mindset.

jokes.text: Are those balls? 'Cause the last time we were here, they were balls.
jokes.setup: Police show Michael photographic evidence
jokes.punchline: Officer refers to previous evidence being blurred genitalia, treating it as routine police work
jokes.explanation: Great callback showing the police have dealt with Bluth family photo evidence before. The casual way he discusses photographed genitalia as normal evidence is perfect deadpan.

jokes.text: I wish they were balls.
jokes.setup: Police clarify this evidence isn't blurred genitalia but actual photographic proof
jokes.punchline: Michael's deadpan wish that he was only dealing with another sexual scandal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael reaction showing how his standards have been lowered by family chaos. Wishing for a sex scandal because it would be less serious than his current situation is peak AD logic.

jokes.text: Right between the gearbox and the 'pull back from aircraft' lever.
jokes.setup: Gob explains where he found Michael's unopened CD
jokes.punchline: He describes finding it in an absurdly specific location with aviation equipment in a regular car
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor suggesting the stair car has aviation controls. The specificity of 'pull back from aircraft' lever in a vehicle context creates surreal comedy.

jokes.text: And guess what else? Dad kissed me. How? He looked pretty unconscious in that picture. I didn't say he was totally into it.
jokes.setup: Gob angrily reveals his father kissed him
jokes.punchline: He admits the kiss wasn't consensual since his father was unconscious
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny escalation that makes Gob's earlier complaint even more disturbing. His casual admission about non-consensual contact while angry adds layers of dysfunction.

jokes.text: Oh, the cousin one? I can't believe it. I was just gonna ask you to go. No, I want to get the whole church group together. We're gonna picket those bastards.
jokes.setup: George Michael mentions the racy cousin movie to Ann
jokes.punchline: Ann's excited response seems romantic until she reveals she wants to organize a protest
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection showing Ann's religious extremism. George Michael's hope for romance crashes into her moral outrage, perfectly capturing their incompatibility.

jokes.text: It's a satire! There's nothing funny about fornication! The bastard saw us!
jokes.setup: Ann's youth group protests Desperate Housewives at the producer's house
jokes.punchline: The producer's defense that it's satire is met with Ann's declaration that sex isn't funny, followed by her swearing when caught
jokes.explanation: Great irony joke showing Ann's hypocrisy. She declares nothing sexual is funny while swearing at a comedy producer, revealing her selective moral standards.

jokes.text: Pop secret? Pop se- Is Oscar my real father?
jokes.setup: Oscar mentions wanting to share his 'Pop Secret' popcorn
jokes.punchline: Buster misinterprets it as a revelation about his paternity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that triggers major plot revelation. The pun on 'Pop Secret' works on multiple levels - brand name, family secret, and Buster's psychological state. Perfect economy and structure.

jokes.text: Your husband's dating Kitty the whore. He is? That's- That's horrible. Do I know my daughter? She only wants what she can't have.
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Lindsay about Tobias dating Kitty
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'horror' is immediately revealed as excitement about wanting someone else's man
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy showing Lindsay's psychology. Lucille's knowing comment about her daughter reveals deep understanding of Lindsay's competitive nature with relationships.

jokes.text: I said 'cot.'
jokes.setup: Lucille threatens Gob with a hard prison bed
jokes.punchline: She clarifies the pronunciation as if that makes the threat less harsh
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille delivery where she focuses on trivial details while making serious threats. Her pronunciation correction doesn't change the cruelty but shows her priorities.

jokes.text: My father is my uncle!
jokes.setup: Buster confronts Lucille about Oscar being his father
jokes.punchline: His dramatic declaration of the family relationship confusion
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic escalation that captures the soap opera nature of family secrets. Buster's earnest delivery of the confusing relationship makes it both dramatic and absurd.

jokes.text: [British Accent] You've ruined the act, Gob.
jokes.setup: Gob decides to turn in his father
jokes.punchline: Franklin suddenly develops a British accent and breaks character to criticize Gob
jokes.explanation: Brilliant surreal moment where the puppet becomes autonomous and critical. The British accent adds absurdist flair while Franklin's moral judgment of Gob creates perfect comedy.

jokes.text: So say good-bye to these, because it's the last time-
jokes.setup: Lucille angrily confronts Oscar
jokes.punchline: She starts to use Kitty's signature move of threatening to expose herself
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Lucille adopting Kitty's signature move. The absurdity of dignified Lucille using this particular threat shows how the family dysfunction spreads.

jokes.text: Yeah. It's the best 52 minutes you'll spend all day.
jokes.setup: Protestors ask if there are more seats at the movie
jokes.punchline: Maeby promotes her terrible movie to its own protestors, and the runtime has shrunk even more
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the movie continuing to shrink while Maeby maintains professional promotion. Her selling to protestors shows Hollywood cynicism about controversy as marketing.

jokes.text: This isn't Marc Cherry's house.
jokes.setup: Ann and George Michael are at the movie protest
jokes.punchline: Ann realizes they're at the wrong location, just like her previous failed protest
jokes.explanation: Callback showing Ann's consistent incompetence at organizing protests. The pattern of being at wrong locations establishes her as ineffective despite her passion.

jokes.text: I don't think we're supposed to shower right now, Bus-
jokes.setup: Buster announces he's going to take a long shower
jokes.punchline: George Michael tries to warn him about the house problems but gets cut off
jokes.explanation: Setup for physical comedy payoff. George Michael's practical concern about the house infrastructure being interrupted foreshadows the coming collapse.

jokes.text: Mommy's gonna take a bath with a bottle of wine.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is drunk and upset about Tobias
jokes.punchline: She announces her plan to drink more alcohol while bathing, in front of her daughter
jokes.explanation: Dark character moment showing Lindsay's poor parenting and self-destructive tendencies. The casual way she announces dangerous drinking to Maeby reveals family dysfunction.

jokes.text: Uh-oh. Gob had hidden his father under a house that had just collapsed.
jokes.setup: Gob realizing where he left his father
jokes.punchline: The casual delivery of potentially fatal consequences
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy delivery of serious situation

jokes.text: Gob had hidden his father under a house that had just collapsed.
jokes.setup: Gob goes to retrieve his father from his hiding place
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the perfect storm of the house collapse with George Sr.'s location
jokes.explanation: Perfect convergence of multiple plot threads. The timing of the house collapse with Gob's father-retrieval mission creates peak dramatic irony and physical comedy.

jokes.text: But he got free- the pipes I chained him to weren't connected to anything.
jokes.setup: Gob explains how George Sr. escaped from under the collapsed house
jokes.punchline: The earlier revealed plumbing problem becomes the solution to his father's predicament
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that recontextualizes the opening plumbing problem as life-saving. The setup from the first scene pays off perfectly, showing the show's masterful plotting.

jokes.text: I'm sick of you needing constant validation for screwing up.
jokes.setup: Gob expects praise for trying to help
jokes.punchline: Michael perfectly diagnoses Gob's psychological pattern of wanting credit for failures
jokes.explanation: Perfect character insight that applies to the entire series. Michael's observation about Gob wanting praise for disasters captures their dynamic and Gob's psychology perfectly.

jokes.text: First, I'm gonna beat the crap out of you, then I'm gonna call an ambulance. Then knowing you, I'm gonna have to wait for some kind of an apology.
jokes.setup: Gob threatens Michael in response to his criticism
jokes.punchline: His threat includes the expectation that even after violence, he'll be the one owed an apology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that perfectly captures Gob's psychology. Even in his revenge fantasy, he expects to be the victim requiring an apology. Perfect structure and character integration.

jokes.text: But there was no time, and getting even with his brother was reward enough.
jokes.setup: George Sr. shaves Oscar's head while he's unconscious
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals George Sr. chose revenge over money, showing his true priorities
jokes.explanation: Dark and brilliant character moment showing George Sr.'s vindictive nature. Choosing revenge over practical reward reveals his true priorities and sets up the twin switch.

jokes.text: Well, I didn't lose him. But he's all puckered and white. On the plus side, you can take him to lunch at the club now.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob lost Franklin
jokes.punchline: Gob explains Franklin is damaged from the ether but notes the racist upside of his new appearance
jokes.explanation: Dark callback showing consequences of Gob's ether plan while highlighting the country club's racism. Gob's casual observation about Franklin's new acceptability is perfectly tone-deaf.
